heroOverview: Microgenia is an underdevelopment or deficiency of the chin in which the mandibular symphysis is abnormally small, posteriorly positioned, or vertically short, producing a chin that is disproportionately recessed relative to the rest of the face. ICD-10 classifies it under dentofacial anomalies as M26.06. The condition ranges from a mild aesthetic concern to a clinically significant skeletal deficiency that co-occurs with Class II malocclusion, obstructive sleep apnea, and temporomandibular joint dysfunction. Microgenia may be developmental, genetic, or acquired — notably as a sequela of condylar damage in childhood from infection, trauma, or radiation that arrests mandibular growth. Treatment ranges from non-surgical orthodontic management for mild cases to chin augmentation implant surgery or skeletal genioplasty for moderate deformities, and full orthognathic surgery when the chin deficiency is part of a broader skeletal Class II jaw relationship. Aesthetic and functional outcomes after surgical correction are generally excellent when treatment is timed after skeletal maturity.

keyStats.prevalence: Precise isolated microgenia prevalence is not reported; skeletal Class II jaw relationships, with which microgenia frequently co-occurs, affect approximately 20-30% of Western populations
keyStats.demographics: Affects all sexes; developmental forms affect both sexes; acquired post-condylar-injury forms predominantly affect children and adolescents who sustained joint damage
keyStats.avgAge: Elective surgery deferred to skeletal maturity: approximately 16-17 years in females, 18-19 years in males
keyStats.globalCases: Global epidemiology specific to microgenia not published; chin augmentation and genioplasty are among the most common facial skeletal procedures worldwide

definition: Microgenia (ICD-10: M26.06) is characterised by a deficiency of chin dimension — horizontal, vertical, or both — relative to established cephalometric norms and the overall facial proportions. The chin point (Pogonion) lies posterior to the normal sagittal limit on lateral cephalometry, the facial lower third is reduced in height, or both. Microgenia must be distinguished from micrognathia, which refers to an overall undersized mandible affecting both the chin and the posterior ramus-condyle complex; microgenia specifically describes isolated anterior mandibular deficiency without necessarily involving the overall jaw size. The two conditions often co-exist, particularly in congenital or growth-disrupted cases. Microgenia falls within the specialty of oral and maxillofacial surgery, craniofacial orthodontics, and plastic surgery. Aetiology is divided into developmental (genetic, idiopathic), congenital (associated with craniofacial syndromes such as Pierre Robin sequence or Treacher Collins syndrome), and acquired (damage to the condyle or mandibular growth centre by trauma, infection, radiation, or juvenile idiopathic arthritis). Functional consequences include Class II dental malocclusion when the entire mandible is retruded, an increased risk of obstructive sleep apnea due to posterior tongue and soft palate positioning, temporomandibular joint strain, and psychosocial distress from perceived facial imbalance. Treatment selection depends on whether the deficiency is isolated to the chin or involves the broader jaw relationship, and on the severity of functional impairment.

typesClassification.description: The chin is deficient in the sagittal plane — recessed relative to the lower lip and nose — without marked reduction in vertical chin height; the most common isolated form.
The chin is abnormally short vertically, producing a reduced lower facial height and a crowded-looking lower third of the face.
Deficiency in both sagittal and vertical dimensions; typically requires genioplasty with anterior advancement and inferior repositioning.

typesClassification.type: Microgenia as part of skeletal Class II relationship
typesClassification.description: Chin deficiency is part of a broader mandibular retrognathia — the entire lower jaw is posteriorly positioned — requiring orthognathic surgery beyond isolated genioplasty.
Unilateral or bilateral condylar damage in childhood arrests mandibular growth, producing progressive chin deficiency and facial asymmetry that worsens until growth ceases.

primarySymptoms: A chin that appears disproportionately small, short, or posteriorly recessed relative to the nose, lips, and forehead when viewed in profile or from the front.
A convex facial profile with the upper lip and nose projecting in front of the lower lip and chin, creating the appearance of a 'weak' or 'receding' chin.
Class II dental malocclusion — upper front teeth sitting prominently in front of lower front teeth — when the chin deficiency is part of a broader mandibular retrognathia.
Difficulty closing the lips at rest without muscular effort, particularly in horizontal microgenia where the chin does not provide adequate support for the lower lip.
Deepening of the labiomental fold — the crease between the lower lip and chin — as the soft tissue bunches over an inadequate bony support.
Retrognenia (posterior positioning) of the soft tissue chin causing the neck-chin angle to appear obtuse and the cervicomental contour to appear less defined.
Temporomandibular joint discomfort, clicking, or headaches from a jaw relationship that distributes load unevenly across the joints.
Snoring or witnessed breathing pauses during sleep from posterior displacement of the tongue base and soft palate in retruded mandibular positions.
Self-consciousness or psychological distress from the perceived weakness or asymmetry of the lower facial profile.
Facial asymmetry with chin deviation to the affected side in unilateral acquired microgenia following condylar growth arrest.
earlyWarningSigns: Progressive recession of the chin relative to the nose and lips during adolescent growth that does not self-correct.
Increasing difficulty achieving comfortable lip closure without visible muscle strain.
A deepening labiomental fold or increasingly obtuse cervicomental angle noticed during orthodontic review.
Chin deviation or facial asymmetry appearing or worsening in a child who sustained a condylar injury earlier in childhood.
emergencySigns: Severe obstructive sleep apnea with significant oxygen desaturation, daytime somnolence, and cardiovascular complications requiring urgent intervention, particularly in infants with micrognathia and Pierre Robin sequence.
Acute temporomandibular joint locking — inability to open the mouth beyond 20-30mm — requiring urgent specialist assessment.

Progressive and unexplained worsening of chin recession in an adult without a history of prior jaw disorder, raising concern for condylar resorption (idiopathic condylar resorption) or other destructive joint pathology.
Acute mandibular fracture in the chin region following facial trauma, requiring emergency maxillofacial assessment.

causes.cause: Developmental underdevelopment of the mandibular symphysis
causes.description: Inadequate growth at the anterior mandibular growth centre during craniofacial development produces dimensional deficiency of the chin; the genetics of this process are incompletely characterised but reflect polygenic control of mandibular morphology with high heritability.

Trauma, septic arthritis, juvenile idiopathic arthritis, or radiation to the mandibular condyle during active growth arrests posterior mandibular growth, preventing the chin from reaching its normal anterior position; unilateral damage produces facial asymmetry, bilateral damage produces symmetric retrognathia and microgenia.

Conditions including Pierre Robin sequence, Treacher Collins syndrome, and Nager syndrome are associated with primary mandibular hypoplasia, of which microgenia and micrognathia are characteristic features; airways management in infancy may be required.

Mandibular morphology is highly heritable; families with skeletal Class II jaw relationships frequently exhibit chin deficiency across generations, with twin studies estimating heritability of mandibular position at 60-80 percent.

Progressive, unexplained resorption of the mandibular condyle, most commonly affecting adolescent females, gradually displaces the mandible and chin posteriorly; the aetiology involves hormonal, mechanical, and vascular factors and can lead to significant progressive microgenia.

Chronic TMJ disease with condylar erosion — from osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, or chronic internal derangement — can progressively reduce mandibular length and chin prominence in adults over years.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of skeletal Class II jaw relationship or weak chin
riskFactors.description: Mandibular retrognathia and chin deficiency are among the more heritable facial characteristics; a first-degree relative with a skeletal Class II bite substantially increases the probability of a similar skeletal pattern.

riskFactors.factor: Condylar injury or disease in childhood
riskFactors.description: Any insult to the mandibular condyle before growth ceases — fracture, septic arthritis, radiation, juvenile idiopathic arthritis — risks permanent growth arrest of the affected side, producing microgenia and asymmetry.

Syndromes with mandibular hypoplasia as a primary feature — Pierre Robin sequence, Treacher Collins, Nager syndrome — produce microgenia and micrognathia that are part of the defined phenotype and require multidisciplinary management from birth.

riskFactors.factor: Female sex in idiopathic condylar resorption
riskFactors.description: Idiopathic condylar resorption overwhelmingly affects adolescent females, particularly in the context of hypoestrogenic states or orthodontic and surgical interventions that alter condylar loading; the cause is multifactorial.

Obesity, nasal obstruction, and upper airway muscle hypotonia exacerbate the impact of a retruded mandible on airway patency; weight management and nasal treatment are modifiable factors that reduce sleep apnea severity.

riskFactors.factor: History of TMJ disease or osteoarthritis of the jaw
riskFactors.description: Chronic destructive TMJ pathology progressively reduces condylar height and mandibular projection, worsening chin recession over years.

affectedDemographics: Adolescents and adults with developmental skeletal Class II mandibular relationships seeking functional or aesthetic correction
Children with congenital craniofacial syndromes associated with mandibular hypoplasia
Adolescent females with idiopathic condylar resorption
Children who sustained condylar injury from trauma, septic arthritis, or radiation before skeletal maturity
Adults with progressive condylar resorption from rheumatoid arthritis or osteoarthritis of the TMJ
Individuals seeking facial aesthetic improvement after identifying chin recession as a primary concern
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of microgenia begins with clinical facial assessment documenting the facial proportions using the rule of thirds and measuring the relationship between chin, lips, and nose in profile. The horizontal microgenia is confirmed when the soft tissue chin point (Pogonion') lies posterior to a vertical line dropped from the lower lip vermilion on profile. Lateral cephalometric radiography is the fundamental diagnostic tool, quantifying the bony chin deficiency by measuring the angle of convexity, the Pg-NB distance, and the lower anterior facial height against population norms. Cone-beam CT provides three-dimensional data for surgical planning and is particularly important in asymmetric or acquired cases. Dental occlusion assessment by an orthodontist determines the dental classification and the need for pre-surgical orthodontics if orthognathic surgery is planned. TMJ clinical examination and, if indicated, MRI of the joint assesses articular disc position and condylar morphology, which is critical when condylar resorption is suspected. Polysomnography is arranged when snoring, witnessed apneas, or daytime sleepiness suggest obstructive sleep apnea. In cases of suspected condylar resorption, single-photon emission CT (SPECT) bone scan confirms whether the process is active. If a congenital syndrome is suspected, genetics review and targeted gene panel testing are initiated.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies the degree of chin deficiency and identifies whether it is part of a broader skeletal Class II jaw relationship, providing the dimensional basis for surgical planning.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standardised side-on skull X-ray taken with the teeth in occlusion in a cephalometric frame; low radiation dose; takes about 1-2 minutes including positioning.

diagnosticTests.test: Cone-beam CT (CBCT) of the mandible and facial skeleton
diagnosticTests.purpose: Provides three-dimensional bony anatomy for virtual surgical planning, particularly important in asymmetric cases and when condylar morphology needs assessment.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 10-20 second scan in a CBCT unit; the rotating arm encircles the head; moderate radiation dose, less than a medical CT; usually performed at an imaging centre attached to the surgical unit.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses articular disc position, condylar morphology, and signs of active resorption, critical when idiopathic condylar resorption or internal derangement is suspected.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You lie in an MRI scanner for 30-45 minutes; no radiation; the scanner is noisy; the images show the TMJ in open and closed mouth positions to assess disc displacement.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Diagnoses obstructive sleep apnea when respiratory symptoms are present, since mandibular retrognathia is a structural risk factor for airway collapse during sleep.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: An overnight study in a sleep lab or using a home device with sensors monitoring brain activity, breathing effort, airflow, oxygen saturation, and body position.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Determines whether condylar resorption is metabolically active before surgical planning, since operating during active resorption leads to relapse of the corrected position.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A radiolabelled tracer is injected into a vein and taken up by metabolically active bone; a scanner detects the tracer in the jaw; the procedure involves low-dose radiation and takes about 3-4 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Defines the current dental occlusion and provides the basis for orthodontic planning both before and after any surgical correction.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Dental impressions or an intraoral digital scan of both arches; non-invasive and performed at an orthodontic appointment.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of microgenia is stratified by severity and whether the chin deficiency is isolated or part of a broader skeletal jaw relationship. Mild aesthetic chin deficiency can be addressed with alloplastic chin implant augmentation — a silicone or porous polyethylene implant placed through a small incision under the chin or intraorally — which is simpler and faster than osteotomy surgery, with recovery in one to two weeks. Moderate to severe bony deficiency requires sliding advancement genioplasty, in which the chin bone is cut and advanced anteriorly, inferiorly, or in both dimensions and fixed with titanium plates and screws; this allows precise three-dimensional repositioning that an implant cannot replicate and achieves permanent stable skeletal change. When microgenia is part of a broader skeletal Class II mandibular retrognathia with dental malocclusion, the complete correction requires combined orthognathic surgery: bilateral sagittal split osteotomy (BSSO) to advance the lower jaw, combined with genioplasty, and framed by pre- and post-surgical orthodontics over a total of twelve to twenty-four months. For patients with obstructive sleep apnea caused by mandibular retrognathia, mandibular advancement surgery is highly effective at improving upper airway dimensions and reducing apnea-hypopnea index, often eliminating the need for continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) therapy. In growing children with condylar growth arrest or syndromic microgenia, mandibular distraction osteogenesis can gradually elongate the mandible through incremental bone transport, avoiding the limitations of acute surgical advancement in immature bone. Mandibular advancement oral appliances worn during sleep are a non-surgical option for managing sleep apnea in adults with mild-to-moderate retrognathia who decline or are not yet ready for surgery.

medicalTreatments.name: Pre-surgical orthodontics
medicalTreatments.description: Orthodontic alignment removes dental compensations that have developed to accommodate the jaw discrepancy — typically upper incisor proclination and lower incisor retroclination — so the dental arches fit correctly after surgical advancement. This phase runs 12-18 months before surgery.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Essential for functional orthognathic outcomes; combined orthodontic-surgical treatment achieves stable Class I occlusion in over 90% of cases.

medicalTreatments.name: Mandibular advancement oral appliance (MAD)
medicalTreatments.description: A custom-fitted splint worn during sleep that holds the lower jaw in a protruded position, increasing upper airway dimensions and reducing apnea-hypopnea frequency. It is the main non-surgical intervention when microgenia contributes to sleep apnea and surgery is not yet indicated.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces apnea-hypopnea index by 50% or more in approximately 60-70% of patients with mild-to-moderate sleep apnea when fitted by a dentist experienced in sleep medicine.

medicalTreatments.name: Post-surgical orthodontics and retention
medicalTreatments.description: After surgical advancement, orthodontic finishing achieves precise occlusal detailing, and retainers maintain the result during bone consolidation. Post-surgical orthodontics typically runs 6-12 months.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieves precise occlusal outcome that surgery alone cannot deliver; retention reduces relapse rates to below 5% in well-planned cases.

medicalTreatments.name: Management of idiopathic condylar resorption
medicalTreatments.description: Active condylar resorption requires stabilisation before corrective surgery. Management includes anti-inflammatory therapy, unloading the joint with a stabilisation splint, and treating co-existing hormonal or inflammatory factors. Surgery is performed only once SPECT imaging confirms the resorption is inactive.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Stabilisation of active resorption is achieved in the majority of cases within 6-18 months; surgical correction after stabilisation has lower relapse rates than surgery during active disease.

surgicalOptions.name: Alloplastic chin implant augmentation
surgicalOptions.description: A silicone or porous polyethylene implant is placed over the chin bone through a small submental or intraoral incision to augment horizontal projection without bone surgery. Simpler and faster than osteotomy but limited in the range and axes of correction it can achieve.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Patient satisfaction rates of 80-90% for aesthetic outcome in appropriately selected cases; implant malposition or resorption requiring revision in approximately 5-10%.

surgicalOptions.name: Sliding advancement genioplasty
surgicalOptions.description: An osteotomy is made below the tooth roots through the chin, the chin bone is advanced anteriorly, inferiorly, or in both dimensions, and fixed with titanium mini-plates. Allows precise three-dimensional repositioning and is preferred over implants for moderate-to-severe deficiency.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Long-term skeletal stability with less than 3mm relapse in over 90% of cases at 2-year follow-up with rigid fixation.

surgicalOptions.name: Bilateral sagittal split osteotomy (BSSO) with genioplasty
surgicalOptions.description: When microgenia is part of a Class II skeletal malocclusion, BSSO advances the entire lower jaw and genioplasty refines any residual chin position. Combined with pre-surgical orthodontics, this achieves both functional occlusion and facial balance.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Greater than 90% long-term skeletal stability when combined with rigid fixation and pre-surgical orthodontics; relapse requiring revision below 5%.

surgicalOptions.name: Mandibular distraction osteogenesis
surgicalOptions.description: Used in growing children or when the advancement required exceeds safe limits for acute surgery; the bone is gradually elongated by incremental traction through an external or internal device over 6-12 weeks, generating new bone in the gap.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Effective for advancing the mandible by greater than 10-15mm; relapse rates of 10-20% over long-term follow-up, higher than acute osteotomy but better than no treatment in severe growth-arrested cases.

alternativeTreatments.name: Orthodontic dental camouflage
alternativeTreatments.description: Dental extractions and retraction of upper front teeth, or advancement of lower front teeth, can reduce the appearance of mandibular retrognathia for mild cases; it does not correct the underlying bony deficiency and has no effect on sleep apnea or TMJ loading.

alternativeTreatments.name: CPAP for sleep apnea
alternativeTreatments.description: Continuous positive airway pressure is the most effective non-surgical treatment for obstructive sleep apnea caused by mandibular retrognathia; it does not correct the jaw relationship but can be used while awaiting or instead of surgical correction.

whySeeSpecialist: Microgenia associated with dental malocclusion, obstructive sleep apnea, or TMJ dysfunction requires assessment by an oral and maxillofacial surgeon and orthodontist working in a joint clinic, because correct diagnosis of the underlying jaw relationship determines whether isolated chin augmentation or comprehensive orthognathic surgery is needed. Operating on the chin alone when there is an underlying skeletal Class II problem can worsen rather than improve function. For growing patients, specialist input from a craniofacial centre is needed to plan timing and sequence of interventions. Sleep apnea from jaw retrognathia should be assessed by a multidisciplinary team including a sleep physician and an oral surgeon experienced in jaw advancement surgery.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek an oral and maxillofacial surgeon with orthognathic surgery expertise, ideally in a combined clinic with an orthodontist. For chin implant surgery, a plastic surgeon with facial surgery experience is appropriate when the jaw relationship is normal. For sleep-apnea-related mandibular retrognathia, look for a centre offering bimaxillary advancement surgery as well as sleep medicine collaboration. If condylar resorption is active or suspected, a TMJ-focused surgeon with access to SPECT imaging and experience in condylar management is essential.
preventionStrategies: Early treatment and immobilisation of condylar fractures in children to reduce the risk of growth arrest and secondary microgenia.
Timely identification and treatment of juvenile idiopathic arthritis involving the TMJ to limit condylar destruction and mandibular growth impairment.
Early diagnosis of congenital craniofacial syndromes allows multidisciplinary planning that includes airway management, feeding support, and timely mandibular distraction if needed.
Orthodontic monitoring of children with known skeletal Class II relationships to plan the optimum timing of surgical correction after growth is complete.
Deferred elective genioplasty until skeletal maturity is confirmed, to prevent growth-related relapse requiring repeat surgery.
lifestyleModifications: Maintain a healthy weight and treat nasal obstruction to reduce the severity of any obstructive sleep apnea component while definitive surgical treatment is planned or awaited.
Use a mandibular advancement oral appliance nightly if prescribed for sleep apnea; consistent use significantly improves sleep quality and reduces cardiovascular risk from sleep-disordered breathing.
Follow a soft diet for six to eight weeks after genioplasty or orthognathic surgery while bone consolidates.
Avoid smoking before and after surgery, as it impairs bone healing and increases infection risk.
Attend all orthodontic appointments to maintain treatment progress and protect the occlusal outcome.

recommended: Soft, easily chewable foods for 6-8 weeks post-surgery while bone healing occurs
High-protein foods to support surgical wound and bone healing
Calcium and vitamin D-rich foods during the bone consolidation period
avoid: Hard, chewy, or sticky foods in the immediate post-operative period that stress healing osteotomy sites
Smoking and excess alcohol, which impair bone healing and increase surgical complication risk

exerciseGuidelines: Regular exercise is encouraged before surgery to optimise physical condition and support recovery. After chin surgery, avoid contact sports and activities with facial trauma risk for at least six weeks. Light walking can usually resume within one to two weeks of isolated genioplasty. After full orthognathic surgery, return to normal physical activity is guided by the surgical team based on bone healing at three to six months. Aerobic exercise has independent benefits for obstructive sleep apnea, reducing apnea severity through improvements in upper airway muscle tone and body weight.
dailyManagement: Take prescribed analgesics and antibiotics for the full post-operative course and attend follow-up appointments to check bone healing and plate integrity.
Rinse gently with chlorhexidine mouthwash twice daily after intraoral procedures to reduce infection risk at the surgical site.
Apply ice packs to the chin for 20 minutes every 2 hours in the first 24-48 hours post-surgery to minimise swelling.
Monitor for warning signs — worsening pain at rest, pus, increasing swelling, or numbness that is not resolving after six weeks — and contact the surgical team promptly.
Wear retainers as instructed after orthodontic treatment to prevent relapse of tooth position and protect the surgical outcome.
Continue using any sleep apnea device as prescribed until the surgical team confirms the correction has resolved the breathing disorder.
prognosis: The prognosis for surgically treated microgenia is excellent when planned and performed after skeletal maturity. Alloplastic chin augmentation and genioplasty both achieve high patient satisfaction — 85 to 95 percent in most published series — when the correct technique is chosen for the degree of deficiency. Genioplasty using rigid titanium fixation has long-term stability with relapse exceeding 3mm in under 10 percent of cases. Combined orthognathic surgery for skeletal Class II relationships achieves stable functional occlusion with satisfaction rates above 90 percent. When mandibular advancement corrects obstructive sleep apnea, the reduction in apnea-hypopnea index is durable and clinically significant, with many patients eliminating their need for CPAP. Acquired microgenia from condylar growth arrest carries a more complex prognosis requiring multistage reconstruction, but functional and aesthetic outcomes with modern techniques are substantially better than without treatment. Psychological outcomes are generally positive: correction of meaningful facial imbalance and sleep apnea is consistently associated with improvements in self-esteem, social confidence, and quality of life.
recoveryTimeline: After isolated chin implant surgery, social presentability returns within five to seven days and full swelling resolves over two to four weeks. After genioplasty, initial swelling resolves over two to three weeks; soft diet for six to eight weeks; bone consolidation confirmed on imaging at three to six months. Lip and chin numbness from mental nerve retraction is common and resolves in most patients within three to six months. After full orthognathic surgery including BSSO, initial swelling persists for four to six weeks; final soft tissue result seen at six to twelve months; orthodontic finishing adds six to twelve months.
complications: Inferior alveolar and mental nerve injury producing numbness or paraesthesia of the lower lip and chin, occurring in 5-20% of genioplasty cases; permanent sensory loss is uncommon, with most patients recovering within six months.

Implant complications in alloplastic augmentation — malposition, infection, implant migration, or bone resorption under the implant — occurring in approximately 5-15% of cases and sometimes requiring implant removal.

Skeletal relapse — gradual return toward the pre-operative chin position — occurring in under 10% of well-planned genioplasty cases; more common in vertical advancements and after condylar resorption.
Recurrence of condylar resorption after surgical correction if the underlying process was not fully stabilised before surgery, resulting in progressive deterioration of the result.
Obstructive sleep apnea persistence or recurrence if mandibular advancement was insufficient or if other contributory factors such as obesity were not addressed.
Psychological dissatisfaction or body dysmorphic disorder in patients with unrealistic expectations, reinforcing the importance of careful pre-operative assessment and expectation management.

supportResources.name: American Association of Oral and Maxillofacial Surgeons — Corrective Jaw Surgery
supportResources.description: Patient-facing overview of jaw correction procedures including genioplasty from the leading U.S. oral and maxillofacial surgery body.

supportResources.name: NHS — Orthognathic (Jaw) Surgery
supportResources.description: Plain-English UK NHS patient guide to jaw correction surgery including genioplasty, covering what to expect before, during, and after surgery.

supportResources.name: Craniofacial Society of Great Britain and Ireland — Patient Information
supportResources.description: Resources for patients and families affected by craniofacial conditions including congenital syndromes associated with mandibular hypoplasia and microgenia.

sources.title: Proffit WR, White RP, Sarver DM. Contemporary Treatment of Dentofacial Deformity. Mosby, 2003
Guyuron B, Michelow BJ, Thomas T. Corrective genioplasty. Part 1: Technique and outcome. Plastic and Reconstructive Surgery, 1995
Sorrenti SJ. Obstructive sleep apnea: mandibular advancement oral appliance versus nasal continuous positive airway pressure in the treatment of mild-moderate sleep apnea. Laryngoscope, 2006
Papadaki ME, Troulis MJ. Mandibular distraction osteogenesis: a review. Seminars in Orthodontics, 2007

confusedWithConditions.name: Macrogenia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Macrogenia is excessive forward or downward development of the chin — too prominent. Microgenia is the opposite — a chin that is too small, recessed, or deficient. Both are chin deformities but require opposite surgical corrections.

confusedWithConditions.name: Temporomandibular joint disorder
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: TMJ disorder is a pain and dysfunction condition of the jaw joint; it can co-occur with the jaw skeletal relationships associated with microgenia but is a distinct clinical diagnosis requiring different management.

confusedWithConditions.name: Sleep bruxism
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Sleep bruxism causes teeth grinding, dental wear, and jaw muscle pain during sleep, and may co-exist with jaw relationship anomalies; it does not cause chin deficiency and is managed primarily by a dentist with night guards.

confusedWithConditions.name: Obstructive sleep apnea
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Obstructive sleep apnea is a breathing disorder during sleep caused by upper airway collapse; it can be caused or worsened by mandibular retrognathia associated with microgenia, but it is a distinct diagnosis requiring its own dedicated treatment.

relatedConditions.name: Macrogenia
relatedConditions.relevance: Macrogenia is the opposite chin deformity — bony excess rather than deficiency — sharing the same anatomical region, diagnostic approach, and surgical correction method (genioplasty) as microgenia.

relatedConditions.name: Temporomandibular joint disorder
relatedConditions.relevance: TMJ dysfunction frequently co-occurs with skeletal jaw discrepancies including microgenia and must be assessed before and after orthognathic surgery to avoid exacerbating joint pathology.

relatedConditions.name: Obstructive sleep apnea
relatedConditions.relevance: Mandibular retrognathia associated with microgenia is a significant structural risk factor for obstructive sleep apnea; mandibular advancement surgery offers a durable anatomical solution for both conditions simultaneously.

relatedConditions.name: Atypical facial pain
relatedConditions.relevance: Facial pain syndromes can co-occur with jaw relationship disorders; assessment for pain generators is part of pre-surgical evaluation to ensure the correct cause is being addressed.

faqs.question: What is microgenia?
faqs.answer: Microgenia is an underdevelopment or deficiency of the chin in which the mandibular symphysis is too small, too short, or posteriorly positioned, making the chin appear recessed or weak relative to the rest of the face. It is classified under dentofacial anomalies (ICD-10: M26.06).

faqs.question: What causes a small or recessed chin?
faqs.answer: The most common cause is genetic — mandibular shape is highly heritable and weak chins cluster in families. Other causes include condylar damage in childhood arresting jaw growth, congenital craniofacial syndromes, idiopathic condylar resorption, and chronic temporomandibular joint disease.

faqs.question: Is microgenia the same as micrognathia?
faqs.answer: No, but they are related. Microgenia specifically refers to a deficiency of the chin point — the anterior mandibular symphysis. Micrognathia refers to an overall undersized mandible. They frequently co-exist, particularly in congenital syndromes, but can be distinguished by cephalometric measurement.

faqs.question: Can a recessed chin cause sleep apnea?
faqs.answer: Yes. When microgenia is part of a broader mandibular retrognathia, the tongue base and soft palate are positioned further posteriorly, narrowing the upper airway and increasing the risk of obstructive sleep apnea. Mandibular advancement surgery can correct both the jaw relationship and the sleep apnea simultaneously.

faqs.question: How is microgenia treated?
faqs.answer: Mild deficiency can be corrected with a chin implant under local or general anaesthesia. Moderate to severe deficiency requires sliding advancement genioplasty, in which the chin bone is cut and moved forward. When the entire jaw is retruded and malocclusion is present, full orthognathic surgery combined with genioplasty is needed.

faqs.question: What is a genioplasty?
faqs.answer: Genioplasty is a surgical procedure in which the chin bone is cut and repositioned. For microgenia, the chin segment is advanced anteriorly and often inferiorly to augment both projection and vertical height, then fixed with titanium mini-plates. It is performed under general anaesthesia through an intraoral incision.

faqs.question: What is the difference between a chin implant and a genioplasty?
faqs.answer: A chin implant places a silicone or porous material over the existing bone to augment soft tissue projection; it is simpler but limited in range. Genioplasty moves the bone itself, allowing precise multi-directional correction that an implant cannot achieve. Genioplasty is preferred for moderate to severe deficiency.

faqs.question: How long is recovery from genioplasty?
faqs.answer: Significant swelling subsides in 2-3 weeks. A soft diet is needed for 6-8 weeks while bone consolidates. Lower lip numbness is common for the first few months and resolves in most patients within 3-6 months. Most people return to non-physical work within 10-14 days.

faqs.question: At what age can microgenia surgery be performed?
faqs.answer: Elective surgery is deferred until skeletal maturity is confirmed — typically 16-17 years in females and 18-19 years in males — to avoid relapse from continued jaw growth after surgery. In infants with severe syndromic microgenia and airway compromise, early distraction is performed as a necessity.

faqs.question: Does microgenia require braces?
faqs.answer: Not always. Isolated chin deficiency without dental malocclusion can be corrected with genioplasty alone without braces. When microgenia is part of a skeletal Class II jaw relationship causing malocclusion, combined orthodontic-surgical treatment with braces before and after surgery achieves a functional bite.

faqs.question: Can microgenia be caused by jaw injury in childhood?
faqs.answer: Yes. Damage to the mandibular condyle from trauma, septic arthritis, radiation, or juvenile idiopathic arthritis during active growth can arrest jaw development, preventing the chin from reaching its normal position. Unilateral damage causes facial asymmetry; bilateral damage causes symmetric retrognathia.

faqs.question: What is idiopathic condylar resorption?
faqs.answer: Idiopathic condylar resorption is progressive, unexplained dissolving of the jaw joint's condyle, most commonly in adolescent females. It gradually moves the lower jaw and chin backwards, worsening microgenia over time. Surgery must be deferred until the resorption is confirmed inactive by imaging.

faqs.question: How is microgenia diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis uses lateral cephalometric radiographs to measure chin position against population norms, cone-beam CT for three-dimensional planning, dental occlusion assessment, and TMJ evaluation. Sleep study and joint MRI are added when sleep apnea or condylar disease are suspected.

faqs.question: Does a weak chin affect appearance significantly?
faqs.answer: Significant microgenia produces a convex facial profile where the chin recedes behind the lips and nose, alters the neck-chin angle, and deepens the fold below the lower lip. The degree of aesthetic impact depends on severity; correction by genioplasty or implant consistently improves self-reported appearance satisfaction.

faqs.question: Is chin implant surgery safe?
faqs.answer: Chin implant surgery is generally safe with a low complication rate in experienced hands. Risks include implant infection (requiring removal in 1-3%), implant migration or asymmetry, bone resorption under the implant over years, and temporary or persistent numbness. Complication rates are higher with alloplastic implants than with genioplasty.

faqs.question: Can orthodontics alone fix microgenia?
faqs.answer: Mild chin recession can be partially camouflaged orthodontically by advancing the lower teeth, but this does not correct the underlying bony deficiency and cannot address functional problems such as sleep apnea. Orthodontic camouflage is appropriate only for mild cases when surgery is declined.

faqs.question: What is distraction osteogenesis for microgenia?
faqs.answer: Distraction osteogenesis gradually elongates the mandible by placing a device across a bone cut and turning it daily to slowly separate the cut ends by 1mm per day; new bone forms in the gap. It is used in growing children or when advancement needed exceeds safe limits for acute surgery.

faqs.question: Can mandibular advancement surgery cure sleep apnea?
faqs.answer: Yes, in carefully selected patients. Advancing the lower jaw increases the airway space behind the tongue and soft palate. Published studies show a median 50-80% reduction in apnea-hypopnea index after bimaxillary advancement surgery, and many patients no longer require CPAP therapy after recovery.

faqs.question: What are the risks of orthognathic surgery for microgenia?
faqs.answer: Risks include temporary or permanent sensory changes of the lower lip and chin from mental nerve retraction, infection, skeletal relapse, malocclusion requiring further orthodontic treatment, and rarely plate removal. Serious complications such as significant haemorrhage or severe infection are uncommon in specialist centres.

faqs.question: What should I do if I think I have a recessed chin?
faqs.answer: Seek assessment from an orthodontist or oral and maxillofacial surgeon who can take a lateral cephalometric X-ray and clinical measurements. They will determine whether the issue is isolated chin deficiency or a broader jaw relationship, assess whether there is functional impact such as sleep apnea, and advise on appropriate treatment.

metaDescription: Microgenia is a small or recessed chin from mandibular bone deficiency. Learn causes, cephalometric diagnosis, chin implant and genioplasty surgery options.
